articles.author: Bridget Brown, Lorian Belanger, Hrvoje Hranjski
articles.description: The U.S. military announced it will begin a blockade of all Iranian ports and coastal areas, tempering President Donald Trump's earlier vow to entirely block the strategic Strait of Hormuz.
articles.title: Live updates: Ship traffic appears to halt in Strait of Hormuz after Trump's blockade announcement

articles.author: Samy Magdy
articles.description: U.S. Central Command says it will begin a blockade of all Iranian ports at 10 a.m. EDT Monday, or 5:30 p.m. in Iran, to be “enforced impartially against vessels of all nations.” But it will allow ships traveling between non-Iranian ports to transit the Strait of Hormuz. That's a step down from an original vow by President Donald Trump to entirely blockade the strait. Early reports are indicating that traffic in the strait has ground to a half since the blockade was announced. The two-week ceasefire remains in doubt. Pakistan, which hosted the talks, says it will seek more dialogue.
articles.title: US military warns it will blockade Iranian ports

articles.author: Will Weissert, Josh Boak, Nicole Winfield
articles.description: Pope Leo XIV has responded to President Donald Trump's criticism over the U.S.-Israel war in Iran, saying the Vatican's peace appeals are rooted in the Gospel. Speaking Monday, Leo emphasized that his message is not a direct attack on Trump but a call for peace. Trump criticized Leo on social media and to reporters, accusing him of being too liberal. Leo suggested over the weekend that a “delusion of omnipotence” is fueling the U.S.-Israel war in Iran. In a social media post, Trump accused Leo of being weak on crime and foreign policy. He also criticized Leo for opposing the U.S. attack on Venezuela.
articles.title: Pope Leo XIV pushes back on Trump over Iran war, citing Gospel

articles.author: Stan Choe
articles.description: Oil prices are back above $100 per barrel after 21 hours of ceasefire talks between the United States and Iran failed to end their war. But U.S. stocks are holding relatively steady Monday, an indicator that Wall Street still hopes both sides will ultimately avoid a worst-case scenario for the global economy. The S&P 500 was virtually unchanged after erasing an earlier dip. The Dow Jones Industrial Average dropped 250 points, and the Nasdaq composite rose 0.3%. The oil market showed more concern, and prices there rose roughly 5%. But even there, prices pared bigger, earlier gains as the morning progressed.
articles.title: Oil rises back above $100, but US stocks hold steadier after US-Iran talks failed to end their war

articles.author: Devi Shastri
articles.description: Some U.S. water utilities are reporting that the Middle East war is disrupting their ability to maintain recommended fluoride levels in the drinking water. Israel is a major supplier of the chemical used to fluoridate drinking water. The number of water utilities affected so far is small, but the shortage is affecting hundreds of thousands of people. The Association of Metropolitan Water Agencies says it expects additional shortages as the war stresses the supply chain. Dentists say a short-term drop in fluoride levels should be fine for most people, but longer-term disruptions could put Americans, especially young children, at higher risk for tooth decay.
articles.title: US fluoride shortage: Iran war is affecting supply

articles.author: Isabel DeBre, Emilio Morenatti
articles.description: A newborn baby struggles in a tent along Beirut’s waterfront. Her mother says Shiman was born 16 days ago there. The family fled Israeli strikes on their home in the southern suburbs as war between Israel and Hezbollah erupted again. The family took refuge in a donated tent. The United Nations says among the 1 million people uprooted by the war in Lebanon are thousands of pregnant women now struggling to access maternal care. The family says the money to pay for the baby's delivery at a public hospital had been buried by an Israeli airstrike on their home.
articles.title: A newborn in a Beirut displacement camp struggles to survive in war

articles.author: Kareem Chehayeb
articles.description: Fierce fighting has erupted in the strategic southern Lebanese town of Bint Jbeil. Israeli troops appeared to encircle the area while Hezbollah militants launched rockets and artillery to push them back. The clashes intensified over the past week after Iran and the United States agreed to a temporary truce. Lebanon and Israel's ambassadors to the U.S. are set to meet Tuesday in Washington for direct negotiations. Israel has scaled back attacks in Lebanon, especially in Beirut, after deadly strikes hit the capital. However, it has stepped up strikes and ground invasions in southern Lebanon.
articles.title: Clashes in Bint Jbeil as Israeli troops encircle Lebanese town

articles.author: Kevin Freking
articles.description: The House Ethics Committee has begun an investigation into whether Rep. Eric Swalwell, D-Calif., engaged in sexual misconduct toward an employee working under his supervision, the panel announced Monday. The announcement comes one day after Swalwell suspended his campaign for California governor after the emergence of allegations that he continues to deny.
articles.title: House Ethics panel opens investigation of Rep. Eric Swalwell

articles.author: Bernard Condon
articles.description: For the Trump family, business is booming. Whereas the Trump Organization didn’t do a single deal overseas in Donald Trump's first term as president, it did eight in the past year. It got more than double the money in four months selling Trump “meme” coins than it did in four years running a massive Washington D.C. hotel. And there are more potential conflicts as the family makes money investing in companies dependent on federal contracts and seeking taxpayer funded grants and loans. While past presidents avoided even the appearance of profiting from their office, some historians worry how Trump's approach might influence future holders of the office.
articles.title: Trump family deal spree could open door for future presidents to profit from office

articles.author: Josh Boak
articles.description: White House economists estimate the United States has a shortage of 10 million houses. That's according to a report out Monday. It says more construction would lower prices, increase home ownership and fuel faster economic growth. President Donald Trump's approval ratings have slumped because of concerns over his handling of the economy. Trump has taken some steps to make it easier to build houses, but he's been slow to embrace other measures. The new report is from the White House Council of Economic Advisers. It lays out a blueprint for how more construction could help the middle class and the economy, setting up an argument Trump could make to voters.
articles.title: How big is the US housing shortage? 10 million homes
